courses.name: The Earth and Life Odyssey: A Journey Through Billions of Years
courses.description: Embark on a thrilling journey through Earth's epic history! Discover the groundbreaking events and powerful forces that shaped our planet, revealing the dramatic story behind the world we live in today.
courses.prerequisites: a 4U/M level in Advanced Functions and at least one of Biology, Chemistry, Earth and Space Sciences or Physics are recommended.  This course is for students who are enrolled in the Faculty of Science.

courses.name: Earth's Epic Tale: A Story Across Billions of Years
courses.description: Embark on a thrilling journey through Earth's epic history! Discover the groundbreaking events and powerful forces that shaped our planet, revealing the dramatic story behind the world we live in today.
courses.prerequisites: a 4U/M level in Advanced Functions and at least one of Biology, Chemistry, Earth and Space Sciences or Physics are recommended. This course is for students who are not enrolled in the Faculty of Science except the Bachelor of Computer Science.

courses.name: Maps, Satellites and the Geospatial Revolution
courses.description: Introduction to the creation and use of maps using a variety of geospatial tools to better understand and resolve physical, social and environmental problems. Overview of geomatics (cartography and map design, geographic information systems, GPS, remote sensing).

courses.name: Planet Hollywood
courses.description: Earth Science concepts and content portrayed in Hollywood films are sometimes accurate but more frequently misrepresented. This course will examine popular Hollywood films to critically evaluate the Earth Science concepts and content that they present and directly compare them to the actual science.

courses.name: Mineralogy to Petrology
courses.description: Chemical, optical and crystallographic properties of common rock-forming minerals, with introduction to common mineral assemblages of igneous, sedimentary, and metamorphic rocks.

courses.name: Geodynamics
courses.description: The structure, composition, and rheological properties of the Earth: lithosphere, mantle and core. Plate tectonics and its relation to geophysical fields, driving mechanisms, and processes at plate boundaries and in plate interiors.

courses.name: Geochemistry
courses.description: This course looks at geochemical processes from deep Earth to surface environments, and the use of geochemical pathways in order to better understand the Earth’s history.

courses.name: Paleontology
courses.description: Introduction to macrofossil and microfossil groups, their paleoenvironmental significance, and principles of evolutionary paleoecology.

courses.name: Sedimentation and Stratigraphy
courses.description: Origin of sediments and their transport, distribution, and primary structures; processes of sediment-to-rock transformation; spatial patterns; controls of stratigraphy; methods of correlation.

courses.name: Paleoecology
courses.description: Introduction to macrofossil and microfossil groups, their paleoenvironmental significance, and principles of evolutionary paleoecology.

courses.name: Dinosaurs
courses.description: A general introduction to dinosaurs, their place in evolution, their social behaviour, the Mesozoic landscape and extinction theories.

courses.name: Climate Change: An Earth Sciences Perspective
courses.description: An exploration of the often dramatic climate changes that have occurred through earth history from a geological perspective, emphasizing the history of earth climates, geological causes of climate change and impact that rapid climate change has had on the biosphere.

courses.name: Introduction to Oceanography
courses.description: An environmental approach to understanding the oceans; introducing the physical and biological aspects of oceanography, marine resources and marine pollution.

courses.name: Engineering Geoscience
courses.description: Applications of the basic concepts of geology, earth materials and earth processes to practical engineering and environmental science. Topics include rock and soil mechanics, slope stability, hydrogeology, geological hazards, and site investigations. Overview of related geophysical methods.
courses.prerequisites: This course is restricted to students in the B.Eng program.

courses.name: Structural Geology
courses.description: Structures and deformation of earth materials.  Topics include stress, strain, folding and faulting.

courses.name: Natural Disasters
courses.description: Physical characteristics and causes of natural disasters of geological origin such as volcanic eruptions, earthquakes, tsunami, landslides, hurricanes and meteor impacts. Discussion on historical perspective, societal impact and mitigation strategies. Emphasis on Canadian case histories.
courses.prerequisites: second-year standing in any degree program. With the exception of the Minor in Earth Sciences, available as a free elective only in any B.Sc. program, including Earth Sciences.

courses.name: On the Origin of Planets
courses.description: Origin and evolution of all planetary objects in the solar system. Topics include the geology of comets, asteroids, the terrestrial planets and rocky moons, Earth's formation and early evolution, ocean worlds, the search for exoplanets and detection of extraterrestrial life.

courses.name: UNESCO World Geoparks and Geoheritage
courses.description: Development of the geologic sciences and enhanced knowledge of the Earth and its history through the lens of inspiring and extraordinary global geological sites that have contributed significantly to science and culture.

courses.name: A Geologic Tour of the National Parks of North America
courses.description: An introduction to the geology of North America's National parks, the ultimate awe-inspiring educational experience, and how these parks collectively tell the story of the processes that have shaped the continent.

courses.name: Drivers of Climate Change through Geological Time
courses.description: A survey of Earth's 4.5-billion-year climate history, focusing on the use of geologic data to understand the drivers of climate change and their impact on the development of the lithosphere, hydrosphere, atmosphere, and biosphere. Course includes experiential learning assignments.

courses.name: Field Geology I
courses.description: Field analysis using geological, geophysical and computational methods leading to the interpretation of the origins of geological features and processes.
courses.prerequisites: ERTH 2314 and ERTH 2407 and permission of the department.

courses.name: Igneous Petrology
courses.description: Origins and evolution of igneous rocks through partial melting, crystallization, degassing, and assimilation of host rocks.  Phase diagrams and classification schemes will be used to provide systematic tools for the description and interpretation of igneous rocks.

courses.name: Vertebrate Evolution: Mammals, Reptiles, and Birds
courses.description: Evolution of mammals, reptiles and birds.  Emphasis on surveying amniote diversity, and the origin of key amniote transformations, as evidenced by the fossil record.

courses.name: Vertebrate Evolution: Fish and Amphibians
courses.description: Evolution of fish and amphibians.  Emphasis on surveying fish and amphibian diversity, and the origin of key transformations of these groups, as evidenced by the fossil record.

courses.name: Geology of Human Origins
courses.description: The origin and evolution of our species from geological, biological and cultural perspectives. The course traces human ancestry from our primate roots through time and changing environments, and explores controversies, frauds, and misperceptions.
courses.prerequisites: any 1000 or 2000 level Earth Sciences or Biology course.

courses.name: Evolution of Mammals, Reptiles and Birds
courses.description: Evolution of mammals, reptiles and birds. Emphasis on surveying amniote diversity, and the origin of key amniote transformations, as evidences by the fossil record.
courses.prerequisites: any 1000- or 2000-level Earth Sciences or Biology course.

courses.name: Evolution of Fish and Amphibians
courses.description: Evolution of fish and amphibians. Emphasis on surveying fish and amphibian diversity and the origin of key transformations of these groups, as evidenced by the fossil record.
courses.prerequisites: any 1000- or 2000-level Earth Sciences or Biology course.

courses.name: Mineral Deposits
courses.description: Analysis and interpretation of the geological and geochemical processes responsible for mineral deposit genesis in a global context.

courses.name: Physical Hydrogeology
courses.description: Principles of deep- to shallow fluid flow within the Earth's crust, and introduction to the exploration, development and management of groundwater as a global resource.

courses.name: Metamorphic Petrology and Processes
courses.description: Genesis of metamorphic rocks as determined from field, petrographic and geochemical data.

courses.name: Geophysical Methods
courses.description: An introduction to the tools of applied geophysics including seismology, electrical, magnetic, and gravitational surveying methods.

courses.name: Isotope Geochemistry and Geochronology
courses.description: This course looks at stable and radiogenic isotope systematics applied to different Earth environments. Students will delve into geochronological techniques and their applications, and apply the principles of elemental and isotopic fractionation to investigate several geological processes.

courses.name: Directed Studies in Earth Sciences
courses.description: One or more projects involving at least 15 days field and/or laboratory research, not related to thesis research. Assessment based on written reports and an oral presentation. Expenses for long-distance travel are borne by the student.
courses.prerequisites: fourth-year standing in any B.Sc. Hons. or Combined Hons. program in Earth Sciences.

courses.name: Special Topics in Earth Sciences
courses.description: Field, laboratory or literature research, not related to thesis research. Assessment based on written reports and an oral presentation. Expenses for travel are borne by the student.
courses.prerequisites: fourth-year standing in any B.Sc. Hons. or Combined Hons. program in Earth Sciences. Major CGPA 8.5 or higher at time of registration for the course.

courses.name: Field Environmental Geobiology
courses.description: Exploration of the relationship between micro- and macro-ecological and evolutionary processes and the Earth's physical and chemical environment. Paleobiology and evolutionary ecology in the context of paleoceanography, paleolimnology and/or paleoclimatology. Will include one or two weeks of field based instruction with costs borne by student.
courses.prerequisites: 2nd year standing in a Faculty of Science program and permission of the Department.

courses.name: Evolutionary Developmental Paleobiology
courses.description: This course explores the mechanistic basis of organismic evolution from genetic, morphogenetic and epigenetic perspectives, within a phylogenetic context of living and extinct vertebrates.

courses.name: Topics in Paleobiology and Evolution
courses.description: This multidisciplinary seminar course investigates various topics in paleobiology, paleoecology and evolutionary theory.
courses.prerequisites: 3rd year standing in any Faculty of Science program.

courses.name: Geotechnical Mechanics
courses.description: Soil composition and soil classification. Soil properties, compaction, seepage and permeability. Concepts of pore water pressure, capillary pressure and hydraulic head. Principle of effective stress, stress-deformation and strength characteristics of soils, consolidation, stress distribution with soils, and settlement. Laboratory testing.

courses.name: Contaminant and Remediation Hydrogeology
courses.description: Geochemical and physical processes controlling contaminant release, migration, and fate in groundwater along with the processes and techniques used for contaminant mitigation and remediation. Examples will include organic and inorganic contaminants in a variety of settings.

courses.name: Environmental Isotopes
courses.description: Isotope applications in environmental and Earth sciences. Emphasis is placed current and novel environmental applications including contaminant tracing, hydrogeological studies, and (bio)geochemical cycling of elements. Mechanisms of stable isotope fractionation and analysis are presented. Student-led projects will explore the broad realm of isotope studies.
courses.prerequisites: CHEM 1001 and CHEM 1002 , and fourth-year standing or permission of the department.

courses.name: Mineral Exploration Field Geology
courses.description: Introduction to the essentials of conducting geological mapping campaign in the Canadian Shield in a field area that has seen considerable industry exploration for volcanogenic massive sulfide mineralization. Activities include outcrop and trench mapping, strain analysis, interpretation of geophysical data, drilling proposals, report writing.

courses.name: Frozen Earth: Unveiling the Snowball Earth Catastrophe
courses.description: Discover how icy cataclysms shaped our planet through Earth’s most extreme climate event: Snowball Earth! We will explore this theory's origins, examine compelling geologic and geochemical evidence, and dive into topics such as glacial sedimentology, the carbon cycle, evolution, and more on this thrilling adventure.
courses.prerequisites: ERTH 2314 or permission of the department.

courses.name: Advanced Sedimentary Geology and Earth History
courses.description: The origin, composition and diagenesis of sedimentary rocks throughout Earth history. Study of modern and ancient sedimentary  systems; development of facies models; petrographic and geochemical analysis of sedimentary rocks.

courses.name: Advanced Petrology
courses.description: Analysis of the physical and chemical conditions, rock-forming processes, as well as the tectonic settings, that control the formation of different rock types. May include one to two weeks of field-based instruction, with costs borne by the student.

courses.name: Physics of the Earth
courses.description: The physical properties of the solid Earth. Gravitational, magnetic and palaeomagnetic fields; seismology and earthquake occurrence; heat flow and thermal history. Geodynamic processes.

courses.name: Field Geology II
courses.description: Field camp integrating advanced field, theory and experimental data. Assessment is based on reports, seminars, and oral examinations. Part of the cost is borne by the student. Departmental funding assistance is available for only one 4000-level field course per student.
courses.prerequisites: completion of the third-year Earth Sciences course requirements and permission of the Department. A supplementary fee will apply.

courses.name: Vertebrate Paleontology Field Camp
courses.description: Field camp extends the student's vertebrate paleontological knowledge by integrating field, theory, and experimental data. Assessment based on written reports and seminars. Part of the cost is borne by the student. Departmental funding assistance is available for only one 4000-level field course per student.
courses.prerequisites: ERTH 3111 or ERTH 3112 , and ERTH 3113 . A Major CGPA of 8.5 or higher and permission of the department. This course is only available to Undergraduate students enrolled in the BSc Earth Sciences with concentration in Vertebrate Paleontology and Paleoecology Honours program.

courses.name: Natural Hazards in Canada
courses.description: Overview of the main natural hazards (such as floods, landslides, forest fires, earthquakes) and severe weather phenomena (such as ice storms, hail, tornadoes) in the Canadian environment. Risk of catastrophic events and their impact on society and infrastructure.
courses.prerequisites: third-year standing in earth science programs or permission of the department.

courses.name: Honours Thesis
courses.description: Independent studies. Requires prior written approval of a topic from a supervisor and the course co-ordinator. Oral and written proposal, progress and defence reports are required.
courses.prerequisites: restricted to B.Sc. Honours and Combined Honours ERTH programs. Major CGPA 8.5 or higher at time of registration for the course.

courses.name: Research in Earth Sciences
courses.description: Understanding research methods, data interpretation and presentation, through readings, seminars and-or laboratory projects related to a topic selected by the student with approval of a faculty advisor.
courses.prerequisites: restricted to B.Sc. Honours and Combined Honours Earth Sciences programs.

free_electives.degree: Earth Sciences with Concentration in Environmental Geosciences B.Sc. Honours (20.5 credits)
Earth Sciences in Vertebrate Paleontology and Paleoecology B.Sc. Honours (20.0 credits)
Earth Sciences in Vertebrate Paleontology and Paleoecology B.Sc. Major (20.0 credits)
Earth Sciences and Physical Geography B.Sc. Combined Honours (20.0 credits)
Biology and Earth Sciences B.Sc. Combined Honours (20.0 credits)

free_electives.text: BIOL (Biology) except BIOL 4810 which may be used only as a free elective for any B.Sc. program. Biochemistry students may use BIOL 2005 only as a free elective.

ERTH (Earth Sciences), except ERTH 2415 which may be used only as a free elective for any B.Sc. program. Students in Earth Sciences programs may use ERTH 2401 , ERTH 2402 , and ERTH 2403 only as free electives.

TSES (Technology, Society, Environment) except TSES 2305 . Biology students may use these courses only as free electives. Integrated Science and Environmental Science students may include these courses in their programs but may not count them as part of the Science Sequence.
BIOC (Biochemistry) except BIOC 1900 which may be used only as a free elective for any B.Sc. program.
BIOL (Biology) except BIOL 4810 which may be used only as a free elective for any B.Sc. program. Biology & Biochemistry students may use BIOL 1010 and BIOL 2005 only as free electives.
ERTH (Earth Sciences) except ERTH 1004 and ERTH 2415 . Earth Sciences students may use ERTH 2401 , ERTH 2402 and ERTH 2403 only as free electives.
TSES (Technology, Society, Environment)  Biology students may use these courses only as free electives.

Any course is allowable as a Free Elective providing it is not prohibited (see below). Students are expected to comply with prerequisite requirements and enrolment restrictions for all courses as published in this Calendar.
Courses Allowable Only as Free Electives in any B.Sc. Program
